title: Specialist Referral Queue Governance
description: A scheduled-care coordination workflow that aligns appointments, dependencies, and staff/resource availability across clinical units. Focus case: specialist referral queue governance.
assumptions: Each referral case is represented by a single T1 instance and follows a single dominant care pathway for scheduling decisions.
Timeslots (T10) and resource objects are exclusive while reserved; reservation removes the object from the available pool until released.
Administrative and clinical availability indicators are authoritative and updated synchronously within the workflow.
External vendor interactions (T9) are modeled as discrete reserve/release operations and may introduce additional latency but are otherwise reliable.
Notifications and manual interventions (coordinator tasks) are available to resolve conflicts not automatically handled by the model.
risks: Race conditions for resource allocation: concurrent assignments could lead to conflicts if external locking is not enforced.
Dependency failure: missing or delayed external vendor/ancillary responses can block appointment creation and increase wait times.
Data inconsistency: incorrect initial assignment or stale availability objects may cause double-booking or missed allocations.
Patient no-shows or late cancellations are not modeled dynamically and can lead to unused reserved resources without additional remediation logic.
Complex approval or escalation logic outside modeled pathways may require human intervention, increasing operational burden and delay.
